text: Probably not that much in the way of updates for me.
think the main, the big thing will be the aftershoots change for us.

I was in San Francisco this week about it, and he agreed.

We're just not a fit for what they're looking for.

And then that'll close by end of August.
So I think very good news, honestly, because it's...
It's nice to know that if a client isn't a good fit, we can potentially end that on our side, although we won't make a habit of it, obviously, only in extreme circumstances.
But yeah, I was just out there hanging with the engineering team last week, and that was good, too, just to meet those folks.
We have a lot of new people in that side, so that should be good for product.
There's a bunch of them are – there's now, like, a full design team, which is interesting.
There's, like, a three-person design engineering team.

So hopefully some of the stuff that we've been waiting for, like the publication automation, will start to come really quickly.
And we're working on other fixes for that, as you may have seen in some of the chats.
But the main one is that Webflow issue, which they had talked about previously, but obviously then just lost track of and didn't solve.
So hopefully we'll reopen that and solve it.
Because it doesn't make any sense for people to be getting booted out of installations just because of how many seats we have or whatever.
Yeah, those are the big updates for me.
I think also continue to give me feedback about the workflows and the generation.
I've talked to Saskia about this a little bit because I did a lot of it last week.

You can put it back to engineering, but like, if you need to run the two processes in parallel right now to get good effects, do that, because they haven't made any substantive changes to the pipelines, so it doesn't make sense to just continue banging your head against the wall until they make changes, and then we can provide more feedback for that.

But you also run every assignment in Atlas, even if you are doing it in exterior tools, too, because that way they'll be able to collect that data and use it to inform future generations of the product.

over to us.

From an editing perspective, too, Ehtisham, like, the first thing that I've been doing with this stuff, because we have, one of the things I've noticed is that, like, Daryl was saying with the writing guidelines, like, I feel like that's where Atlas is struggling almost the most, is, like, to apply that.
And doing it in cloud, I ask it to give me, like, a report, and basically, like, hey, what would you change?
What needs changing to align?
But these writing guidelines, it tells you, and then you can be like, okay, do this, but don't do

That, this, but don't change that, or just, hey, yeah, go for it, change the whole thing, and it'll keep everything else the same, which I wish, I wish Atlas did that, like, I just want to be able to tell it, like, keep everything else the same, just change these things, you know, and so, anyway, I've had really good luck with that.

With Aimbid, like, with Aimbid, what I'm doing is I'm using Gemini, and I, like, I gave it instructions, like, go familiarize yourself with Aimbid.io, and then make sure you look up the latest technical information in this sector, blah, blah, blah, then I gave it competitors, and then I started tweeting it the previously done articles that we have to revise, and then Gemini is kind of correcting them for me, the code and the technical stuff.
But it's still, like, an AI-generated output, I still don't know how accurate it is.
we'll have to see what they think about it, but both Cloud and Gemini are supposed to be highly technical.
So hopefully that helps.

find that too, like Gemini is really good if you're already in Google Docs, if you want it to do something and you're like, hey, this section doesn't sound like the rest of the article, align it with it or something.
